natural_occurrence.description: While the raw materials are natural plant materials, vegetable carbon is produced through high-temperature carbonization processes. The product is essentially pure carbon derived from incomplete combustion of vegetable matter.

manufacturing.description: Produced by carbonization of vegetable materials (wood, coconut shells, bamboo, peat, or other plant residues) at high temperatures in a limited supply of air, followed by steam activation. The process involves controlled incomplete combustion that converts organic plant matter into finely divided elemental carbon particles. This is literally burnt plant material processed into a stable black pigment.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used in cosmetics and personal care products for black coloring
other_uses.industrial: Used in inks, paints, and industrial applications
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Used as a colorant in pharmaceutical preparations and activated charcoal products

eu.conditions: Must meet strict purity standards; carcinogenic PAHs must be <1.0 µg/kg (as benzo[a]pyrene)

usa.notes: FDA does not approve E153 for use in food

japan.notes: Classified as existing food additive under Japanese regulations

australia_nz.notes: Approved as Carbon blacks or Vegetable carbon

canada.notes: Canadian Government concluded in 2011 that carbon black could continue to be used in food packaging and consumer products

regulatory_history.event: JECFA evaluates vegetable carbon but does not establish ADI
Canada reviews carbon black and concludes it can continue to be used in consumer products
EFSA re-evaluates E153; Panel considers data too limited to establish ADI but concludes not of safety concern when PAH levels <1.0 µg/kg
EFSA publishes scientific opinion on amendment of specifications for vegetable carbon
